channel.title: Yahoo News – World
channel.description: Latest world news from Yahoo News

item.title: North Korea says Xi's visit produced 'far-reaching blueprint' for ties
item.description: The leaders of North Korea and China adopted a "far-reaching blueprint" for bilateral ties during Xi Jinping's recent visit to Pyongyang, the state-run Korean Central News Agency (KCNA) said on Wednesday.On Tuesday, China's state-run Xinhua news agency reported Xi as saying he had reached "an important consensus with Kim on developing China-DPRK relations in the new era", using North Korea's official acronym.
item.dc:creator: AFP

item.title: Iran’s expat community sees chance to confront regime — at World Cup
item.description: On a warm Sunday morning in Inglewood, more than 200 Iranian-Americans gathered outside SoFi Stadium with red, white and green flags draped around their shoulders — the lion and the sun in the center, the way it was before 1979.
item.dc:creator: California Post

item.title: Pope Leo draws 40,000 at Barcelona vigil, addresses crowd in Catalan
item.description: Pope Leo XIV addressed about 40,000 mostly young people at a prayer vigil in Barcelona's Olympic Stadium on Tuesday, speaking in both Catalan and Spanish during the second leg of his week-long visit to Spain. The pope, a native English speaker from the United States who is fluent in Spanish after years serving as a missionary and bishop in Peru, received an enthusiastic response when he used Catalan, the regional language of north-eastern Spain's Catalonia region.
item.dc:creator: dpa

item.title: Brazil intercepts 108 Cuban immigrants amid growing asylum applications
item.description: Last year, for the first time in a decade, Cuban asylum applications exceeded Venezuelan ones in sign of growing strain.

item.description: Was the person injured in what news media described as an attempted beheading in Belfast as young as 15 years old? No, that's not true: The authorities said the victim was a man in his 40s. He was in the...

item.title: China's Destroyer Fleet Production Could Soon Outpace The US Navy
item.description: Think the U.S. military still has the largest maritime force? Discover why defense analysts are sounding the alarm over China's new destroyer fleet.

item.title: LISTEN: Brian Hooker's own words appear to place him in the water the night missing wife vanished in Bahamas
item.description: A recorded phone call from Brian Hooker days after wife Lynette vanished in the Bahamas suggests he was in the water with her, contradicting his account.

item.title: Mexico blocks most imports of live animals from US after screwworm cases
item.description: MEXICO CITY, June 9 (Reuters) - Mexico will stop ‌most imports of live animals ‌from the U. after cases of ​the flesh-eating screwworm pest were confirmed in Texas and New Mexico, Mexico's agriculture ministry said on Tuesday.

item.title: Iran threatens counterstrikes after US attacks
item.description: Iran has threatened counterstrikes after US attacks on targets in Iran in response to the downing of a US military helicopter. "Our Powerful Armed Forces will leave no attack or threat unanswered," Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi wrote on X early on Wednesday.

item.title: Catalonia's famed human tower climbers greet Pope Leo in Barcelona
item.description: Bruna Vall Galán, 8, gave a unique welcome to Catalonia to Pope Leo XIV Tuesday night — from the top of a nearly 10-meter (33-foot) human tower created at the start of Leo's prayer vigil in Barcelona. Human towers, or “castells” in the Catalan language, are not only a feat of equilibrium, strength and teamwork, but a crucial part of the proud identity in this northeastern Spanish region. One of the most celebrated groups to uphold this tradition, the Castellers de Vilafranca, was tapped to perform for the pope during his seven-day trip to Spain and allowed The Associated Press to tag along on their journey, from the long bus ride to the backstage jitters to the sweaty, grinning high-fives after Leo's applause.

item.title: North Korea says Xi's visit produced 'far-reaching blueprint' for ties
item.description: The leaders of North Korea and China have adopted a "far-reaching blueprint" for bilateral ties during Xi Jinping's recent visit to Pyongyang, the Korean Central News Agency said Wednesday."Comrade Kim Jong Un and Comrade Xi Jinping expressed satisfaction and deep emotion over the fact that they provided a far-reaching blueprint for the development of the relations," KCNA, the country's official news agency reported.

item.title: Israel Police arrest seven individuals on suspicion of running human trafficking, blackmail ring
item.description: According to Israel Police, the suspects lured the women in with false promises of office jobs and provided them housing before forcing them to provide sexual services. Israel Police arrested seven individuals suspected of running a human trafficking ring, exploiting young women, and blackmail, police announced on Monday.

item.title: Family in bid to find mum's Polish stem cell donor for second transfusion
item.description: Lisa Semple, 58, was treated for leukaemia last year with a stem cell transplant from a 19-year-old man.

item.title: Lebanon says 11 dead in Israeli strikes on Tyre
item.description: Israeli airstrikes on the historic city of Tyre in southern Lebanon and surrounding areas killed 11 people Tuesday amid an Israeli military warning for the entire city to evacuate.An Israeli strike near Tyre killed three people in the southern Lebanese city on Tuesday, the health ministry said, taking the death toll up to 11 in the region.

item.title: Protests across Scotland follow Belfast knife attack
item.description: Hundreds of demonstrators gathered at locations across the central belt with their faces covered and many holding Union flags and banners.

item.title: Inside Myanmar, rebels are losing ground as military forces men into army
item.description: The BBC travels with rebels to frontline positions in Myanmar to see how the war is unfolding.

item.title: ‘I Grew Up Trying To Explain the Truth About Albania—the Sazan Island Protests Are About So Much More Than Ivanka and Jared’s Resort’
item.description: I've spent years explaining Albania's beauty—but protests against Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner’s resort plans reveal a deeper truth.

item.title: Colombia passes law to track cattle and keep deforestation-linked beef out of supply chains
item.description: Colombia has enacted a landmark law requiring the cattle industry to trace livestock and prove beef supply chains are free from deforestation, a measure environmental groups say makes it the first tropical forest country to adopt such a nationwide framework. The law requires government agencies and private companies to integrate cattle-tracking, land ownership and deforestation-monitoring systems to identify livestock linked to forest loss and keep them out of supply chains.

item.title: Analysis: Iran, Israel use Lebanon to test deterrence, negotiating power
item.description: As Israel sought to impose new rules of engagement, threatening to strike the southern Lebanon suburbs, Iran could not remain silent and took action.

item.title: UK and allies sanction 'networks' enabling settler violence in West Bank
item.description: France will also bar far-right Israeli Minister Bezalel Smotrich from entry as part of the measures, which Israel condemns as "disgraceful".

item.title: Canadian personalities targeted by fake story on clearing school lunch debt
item.description: Clickbait circulating on Facebook claimed famous Canadians -- from former prime minister Justin Trudeau to current Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre -- donated hundreds of thousands of dollars to clear school lunch debt in the country. This is false; a researcher told AFP Canadian families are not racking up unpaid obligations to school cafeterias, as is the case in the United States, and the articles shared as proof appear to be mass-produced with artificial intelligence.

item.title: Why "pacifist" Japan has China worried
item.description: Japan is positioning itself as a focal point for defense cooperation among U.S. partners.

item.title: Firefighter’s Son Leads Mother and Baby Sibling to Safety During 7.8-Magnitude Philippines Earthquake
item.description: His firefighter dad was on duty. He stepped up, kept everyone safe, and wants the uniform someday.

item.title: Pope's youth rally in Spain gets raw, with frank discussion of depression and domestic violence
item.description: Pope Leo XIV on Tuesday sought to encourage Spain’s young people to persevere in their faith, as he presided over an evening rally that was notable for its frank discussion of depression, domestic violence and “toxic” family relationships. The U.S.-born pope received a raucous welcome at the Olympic stadium in Barcelona, the second stop of his weeklong visit to Spain that has drawn huge crowds despite the country’s strong secular bent. The event featured several nods to Catalan culture, including a demonstration of the region’s famed human tower acrobats, known as castellers.

item.title: Global conflicts at highest level since WWII
item.description: The number of global conflicts was at a record high of 65 in 2025. Of those 65, 13 of them escalated to war.

item.title: India increases nuclear arsenal to almost same size as Britain’s
item.description: India has expanded its arsenal of nuclear weapons while developing new delivery systems capable of reaching targets in China and across Pakistan.

item.title: Bystanders hailed as 'heroic' after intervening in brutal knife attack by Sudanese migrant in UK
item.description: A Sudanese man was arrested on suspicion of attempted murder after a knife attack in Belfast, Northern Ireland left a man in his 40s with serious injuries.

item.title: A Sherpa Went Missing in Mount Everest's 'Death Zone.' Funeral Rites Had Begun. Then the Unthinkable Happened
item.description: This story has all the makings of a future Hollywood blockbuster. Just think: a popular sherpa went missing in Mount Everest's so-called "death zone." He was presumed dead, so much so that funeral rites had already started. Then, out of nowhere, he's found alive. The Nepali guide is Dawa Sherpa. He ...

item.title: Protesters block road to Mexican World Cup stadium
item.description: Thousands of demonstrators blocked an avenue leading to Mexico City's Azteca Stadium on Tuesday, just days before the 2026 World Cup kicks off at the venue.The teachers have also set up camp near the World Cup fan zone in Mexico City's Zocalo square.

item.title: Brazilian police rescue 108 Cuban migrants at the northern border and arrest 5 alleged smugglers
item.description: More than 100 Cuban migrants attempting to enter Brazil on its northern border with Guyana were rescued from human smugglers, Brazilian police said. The 108 migrants were in custody in Roraima state while authorities regularize their immigration status before referring them to social services workers, police said Tuesday. Police also said they arrested five people on migrant smuggling charges.

item.title: Car bomb kills man near Moscow amid speculation over his military rank
item.description: A man has been killed by a car bomb near the Russian capital Moscow, with unconfirmed reports suggesting the victim was a colonel or even a general in the Russian rocket forces, Ukrainian and Russian bloggers suggested on Tuesday. The explosion occurred in a moving BMW at around 5:30 am (0230 GMT), the Investigative Committee of Russia said, adding that proceedings had been opened without specifying the direction of the investigation.

item.description: Bolivian farmers and coca growers' organizations have formally threatened to seize military and police facilities in the Cochabamba Tropics region.

item.description: Two days before the World Cup opening match between Mexico and South Africa, massive protest actions have put security forces in the host city of Mexico City on high alert. Police discovered a total ...

item.title: Pope Leo meets Latin superstar Bad Bunny on sidelines of Spain visit
item.description: Pope Leo XIV met Latin superstar Bad Bunny on the sidelines of his visit to Spain, with the private encounter taking place after a Madrid church event at the Santiago Bernabéu football stadium, Vatican spokesman Matteo Bruni confirmed on Tuesday. Bruni said Leo and the Puerto Rican singer had spoken late on Monday evening "shortly before he [the pope] left the stadium," confirming earlier media reports.

item.title: Thousands of Dutch homeowners told to rethink EV charging and heat pump plans as grid jams worsen
item.description: Electrification can often lower energy costs over time, but local infrastructure must be able to support that shift.

item.title: ‘Hey, this box stinks’: the weird work of intercepting trafficked wildlife in Alaska
item.description: Chris Andrews was working the belt at the Anchorage airport last fall, watching international cargo arrive. “An employee said, ‘Hey, this box stinks, Chris,’” Andrews recalled. The box was labeled “car parts.”

item.title: This Is The Corsair Drone Boat That Plucked The Downed Apache Crew Out Of The Gulf Of Oman
item.description: The U.S. Navy's top drone task force in the Middle East only started operating the Corsair uncrewed surface vessels in March.

item.title: There Are Only 16 (Out Of 186) World Leaders Older Than Trump
item.description: Trump will turn 80 on Sunday.

item.title: Inside Ukraine’s AI-Enabled Drone Campaign Targeting Russian Logistics Deep Behind The Lines
item.description: An officer assigned to the "mid-range" drone campaign gives unprecedented insights into its strikes at the heart of Russian logistics.

item.title: Colombian presidential candidate urges prosecutors to investigate alleged voter coercion
item.description: Colombian presidential candidate Abelardo de la Espriella on Tuesday urged prosecutors to investigate whether rebel groups coerced citizens in remote municipalities into voting for governing party candidate Sen. Iván Cepeda during the May 31 first-round presidential election. De la Espriella’s campaign issued a statement saying that Cepeda secured more than 70% of the vote across 109 municipalities with active illegal armed groups, reaching as high as 97% in some locations.

item.title: 'Beaten to death': the grim toll of Ecuador's security crackdown
item.description: Three months after her son died at the hands of Ecuador's military, Monica Franco is still inconsolable.Monica cannot understand why, if her soccer-loving son was suspected of a crime, the soldiers "didn't take him to the police."

item.title: Zamir says strike in Iran was preparation for a heavier blow, will return to bombing if needed
item.description: "We are ready to strike Iran again with another hard and deep blow. Iran’s attempt to set equations and change reality will fail," Zamir said.

item.title: Murder of Lyhanna: Anger grows as thousands take to the streets in Paris
item.description: Thousands of people gathered in Paris to demand accountability and reforms after the killing of 11-year-old Lyhanna, criticising the handling of the case by French authorities and the justice system.

item.title: Ukraine winning war with Russia, retired U.S. generals say
item.description: Several retired U.S. generals and the former director of a U.S. intelligence agency told CBS News they believe Ukraine now has the upper hand in the war with Russia.

item.title: Nigeria’s conflict-hit Borno state battles cholera outbreak that has killed 74
item.description: A cholera outbreak in northeastern Nigeria has killed 74 people and infected more than 7,000 others since it started in early May, medical charity Doctors Without Borders said Tuesday. The outbreak, reported in 14 of Borno state’s 27 local governments, is unfolding in communities with health systems made fragile by nearly two decades of violent extremism from the Boko Haram insurgent group.

item.title: Deaths reported amid protest crackdown in Pakistani-controlled Kashmir
item.description: Security forces have violently cracked down on protests in Pakistani-controlled Kashmir, with several people killed and dozens injured, Amnesty International reported on Tuesday. The human rights organization reported an internet blackout, mass detentions and deadly violence.

item.title: World Cup opening match facing protests over 130,000 missing Mexicans
item.description: The World Cup’s opening match faces protest chaos as thousands march over Mexico’s crisis of 130,000 missing people and worker conditions.

item.title: Sailor fails in attempt to cross Atlantic in 4ft boat
item.description: A British sailor has abandoned his attempt to cross the Atlantic in a 4ft boat after he needed rescuing just two days into the voyage.

item.title: There’s a Terrible Reason Why This Ebola Outbreak Is Different
item.description: It wouldn’t have been this way a few years ago.

item.title: Peru election chief tells AFP count could take two weeks
item.description: The head of Peru's election authority told AFP that the results of Sunday's presidential election may not be known for a fortnight, dashing hopes of a quick resolution to the ultra-tight race.The first round presidential election result took more than 30 days to complete. cm/ljc/arb/bgs

item.title: Ravenstorm At The Center Of Airbus’s New Combat Drone Portfolio
item.description: As Europe’s flagship next-generation fighter effort unravels, Airbus is betting heavily on uncrewed collaborative combat aircraft.

item.title: Tunisian court sentences prominent journalist Boukrim in absentia to four years
item.description: June 9 () - A Tunisian court sentenced prominent journalist Khaoula Boukrim to four years in prison in absentia, she said on Tuesday, in ‌a ruling critics say highlights a growing crackdown on critical voices ‌and free speech under President Kais Saied. The ruling against Boukrim is the latest move against journalists ​following the jailing earlier this year of several media figures, including Zied Heni, Mourad Zghidi and Borhen Bsaies.

item.title: Iran accelerates execution campaign against anti-regime activists amid internet censorship
item.description: Iran accelerates executions of dissidents and protesters amid internet censorship, with actual numbers almost certainly higher than documented figures.

item.title: Ukraine is close to cutting Putin’s supply lines to Crimea
item.description: I recently visited Ukraine’s 3rd Assault Corps, perhaps the world’s leading military force in the realm of remote-controlled warfare. I witnessed its surveillance and attack drones in action and under construction plus the training of drone pilots. Another system pioneered by the Corps is unmanned ground vehicles, which are utilised extensively for assault operations as well as fuel, water and ammunition supply and casualty evacuation.

item.title: Bodies of 119 shipwrecked sailors from Nelson’s fleet to be exhumed
item.description: The remains of 119 drowned sailors could be exhumed to protect them from being exposed by coastal erosion.

item.title: Deaths reported amid protest crackdown in Pakistani-controlled Kashmir
item.description: Security forces have violently cracked down on protests in Pakistani-controlled Kashmir, with several people killed and dozens injured, Amnesty International reported on Tuesday. The human rights organization reported an internet blackout, mass detentions and deadly violence.

item.title: Suspicions of arson strengthen over fire at German substation
item.description: Investigators have found more evidence that a large-scale power outage that hit the western German city of Reutlingen on Monday was caused by arson. They are now assuming that the substation fire that caused the power cut was started deliberately, based on the preliminary findings of the investigation so far, the Stuttgart Public Prosecutor’s Office and the Baden-Württemberg State Criminal Police Office said in a statement on Tuesday.

item.title: Israel preventing more than 16,500 Palestinians from accessing medical treatment
item.description: Israel has systematically destroyed Gaza’s healthcare system since launching its genocidal war in October 2023.

item.description: The man has also been charged with possession of an article with blade in a public place and threats to kill.

item.description: War damage and blockade have worsened economic conditions for many struggling Iranians.

item.title: Paraglider Captured Spiraling to His Death. First Responders Found His Body in the Woods
item.description: "Death is certain, life is not," wrote Danilo José de Lima before his death. "Make it count"

item.title: Explosive devices seized at teachers' protest in Mexico City
item.description: Police have seized explosives during a teachers’ protests in Mexico City. The Interior Ministry announced on Tuesday that 59 explosive devices had been discovered on a bus carrying arriving demonstrators.

item.title: Tyre residents flee to beaches as Israeli warning empties city
item.description: The beaches of the southern Lebanese port city of Tyre, usually crowded with summer visitors, turned into makeshift refuges on Tuesday as some families fled their homes following an Israeli evacuation warning. Families carrying bags, blankets and plastic chairs streamed toward the seafront, seeking open spaces they hoped would be safer from airstrikes.

item.description: By Tariq Maqbool MUZAFFARABAD, June 9 (Reuters) - Cities and towns across Pakistan-administered Kashmir were brought to a standstill on Tuesday after protesters clashed with security forces ahead of a

item.title: AH-64 Apache Shot Down By Iran, U.S. Will Retaliate: Trump
item.description: The AH-64 crashed into the Gulf of Oman and its crew was rescued by a drone boat, and now Trump says it was due to enemy action.

item.title: Poland and Ukraine on collision course over historical dispute
item.description: A historical dispute between Warsaw and Kiev continues to smoulder unresolved, dividing Polish foreign policy. Prime Minister Donald Tusk said in Warsaw on Tuesday that the Ukrainian side must take responsibility for the crisis and, consequently, for finding a way out of it.

item.title: Zelensky meets Baltic and Nordic state leaders in Estonia
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on Tuesday met the leaders of the Nordic and Baltic States in Estonia, where he attended the summit of the Nordic-Baltic Eight (NB8) countries as a guest. In the capital Tallinn, Zelensky also held bilateral meetings with some of the prime ministers and with Estonian President Alar Karis.

item.title: Big Ben climb cost taxpayers £67,000, court told
item.description: Daniel Day denies intentionally or recklessly causing a public nuisance by scaling the Elizabeth Tower.

item.title: Militants and police executed and maimed dozens of Palestinians in Gaza, UN report says
item.description: Hamas militants and police units in Gaza beat, maimed and publicly executed dozens of Palestinians during its war with Israel in acts amounting to war crimes, according to a United Nations report released Tuesday. The U.N. Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights' report documented hundreds of cases of extrajudicial punishment in the war-ravaged territory, which it said were often publicized during and afterward to instill fear in the populace. “These cases involved executions, kneecapping, bone-breaking with metal pipes or cement bricks and beatings and were framed by the perpetrators as punishments for alleged collaboration with Israel, looting humanitarian aid, theft, drug-related offenses or affiliations with internal rivals,” it said.

item.title: Israeli warnings trigger mass exodus from Lebanon’s historic Tyre
item.description: Thousands of residents fled Lebanon's southern city of Tyre on 9 June, with long queues of vehicles heading north after Israeli military warnings heightened fears of further air strikes and a wider regional escalation.

item.title: Drone boat rescues U.S. helicopter crew shot down by Iran
item.description: The crew of a U.S. Apache attack helicopter shot down by Iran was rescued by a drone-boat known as Corsair.

item.title: Afghan Taliban forces open fire at protest sparked by dress code arrests
item.description: Afghanistan's Taliban rulers deny shooting at protesters and claim no women or girls have been arrested because they're all following strict dress code rules.

item.title: Will Iran-Israel ‘tit-for-tat’ flare ups become the new norm?
item.description: Iran’s goal is to up the ante in the region, such that it feels it can do what it wants, when it wants. It has been doing this already for 45 years.

item.title: UK, France and other Western nations issue new sanctions on Israeli settlers in the West Bank
item.description: A coalition of six countries on Tuesday issued new joint sanctions on Israeli settlers and settlements in the West Bank, including a hard-line Israeli cabinet minister already sanctioned by Western countries, escalating pressure on Israel over a wave of violence against Palestinians in the occupied territory. The top diplomats of Australia, the United Kingdom, Canada, France, Norway, and New Zealand announced the moves in a joint statement. “Extremist violent settlers, with the backing of their supporters, continue to attack Palestinians and abuse their human rights,” they said.

item.title: Mexico City suspends classes, shifts to remote work for World Cup kickoff
item.description: MEXICO CITY, June 9 (Reuters) - Mexico's President Claudia Sheinbaum on Tuesday issued a decree ordering federal workers in the capital to work from home on June 11 and suspending school classes to

item.title: Israeli minister lashes out at Italy in Gaza flotilla ‘torture’ row
item.description: Itamar Ben-Gvir, Israel’s extremist national security minister, has described Italy as the “land of the flip-flop”, escalating a diplomatic row over the Gaza flotilla.

item.title: Peru's presidential runoff shows a razor-thin gap between candidates
item.description: The winner will be the South American country’s ninth president in 10 years. Official figures showed nationalist congressman Roberto Sánchez with 50.055% of votes, while conservative politician Keiko Fujimori had 49.945%. Fujimori, the daughter of a disgraced former president, and Sánchez, an ally of an imprisoned ex-president, beat 33 other candidates in the initial vote in April, but neither earned even 20% of support.

item.title: North Korean Hackers—Posing As Fake IT Workers—Behind Nearly Half Of All Tech Firm Attacks, Report Says
item.description: One North Korean group accounted for nearly half of all state-sponsored hacks on tech companies, a report found.

item.title: Alleged rapist ‘asked Pelicot for advice on assaulting partner’
item.description: A man sought advice from Dominique Pelicot before drugging and raping his own partner, French police have said.

item.title: Two reportedly killed as women take part in rare protest in Afghanistan
item.description: The demonstration, in the western city of Herat, was broken up after police are alleged to have opened fire.

item.title: Spain: Thousands Gather as Pope Leo XIV Reaches Barcelona
item.description: Spain's Barcelona welcomed Pope Leo XIV on 9 June as thousands gathered for a prayer service at the city's cathedral of the Holy Cross and Saint Eulalia. The pontiff's visit includes a landmark ceremony at the Sagrada Familia Basilica.

item.title: Watch: Skunk spotted wandering loose on Scottish road
item.description: Travelers on a road in Scotland were surprised to capture video of a non-native animal on the loose: a skunk.

item.title: Pope Leo XIV met Bad Bunny in Madrid on Monday: Vatican
item.description: Pope Leo XIV held a surprise meeting with Puerto Rican music star Bad Bunny at Real Madrid's Bernabeu stadium during his state visit to Spain, the Vatican said on Tuesday.Around 80,000 people packed the iconic football ground for a meeting between the pope and Madrid's diocesan community on day three of Leo's trip to Spain.

item.title: Putin helped North Korea back from the brink – and Xi is watching closely
item.description: In Kim Jong-un’s own words, North Korea has undergone a “miraculous transformation”.

item.title: AP Interview: Albania's leader defends Kushner-linked luxury development
item.description: Albanian Prime Minister Edi Rama is vowing to press ahead with a luxury development linked to U.S. President Donald Trump ’s son-in-law Jared Kushner, despite a surge in protests against it there. In an interview with The Associated Press on Tuesday, Rama dismissed environmental objections as the result of misinformation and said the development was turning Albania from a country once ignored by investors into one “where the big capital wants to come and the big investors want to come." The government says the development would be transformational for the former communist nation as it seeks to enter the high-end tourism market and pushes for European Union membership.

item.title: New WWI memorial unveiled to remember lost heroes
item.description: The addition to Brookwood Military Cemetery honours more than 400 forgotten service men and women.

item.title: Australia Moves Toward World-First THC Driving Limit for Medicinal Cannabis Users
item.description: New South Wales could become the first jurisdiction to set a legal THC limit for medicinal cannabis drivers, allowing patients with prescriptions to drive while introducing warnings and penalties for those exceeding the threshold.

item.title: Why Jared And Ivanka’s Island Project Could Ruin Albania’s Bid To Join EU
item.description: The European Commission has warned the Albanian government about its efforts to join the EU.

item.title: Escaped African serval found under British Columbia porch
item.description: Police in Vancouver, British Columbia, responded to a home where residents had spotted an unusual animal under their porch: an African serval.

item.title: French air force soars through NY skies in Statue of Liberty flyover
item.description: France's air force acrobatics team staged a flyover of the Statue of Liberty on Tuesday, part of commemorations for the upcoming 250th anniversary of the US Declaration of Independence.Several other flyovers are planned during this deployment -- which ends on July 5 -- in Maryland, Virginia, over Washington, DC, and again over New York for the final day of festivities on July 4, the anniversary of the Declaration of Independence. rh-gw/msp

item.title: 'EU's environmental policy must be part of defence strategy,' Commissioner Roswall says
item.description: The European Environment Commissioner told Euronews that Brussels must "connect the dots" and understand that environmental, economic and security interests go hand-in-hand.

item.title: Nearly 100,000 pres. pardon applicants’ records exposed to wartime cyber threats, report finds
item.description: Nearly 100,000 pardon applicants’ records were held in systems that did not fully meet legal information-security requirements, while wartime cyber risks intensified. Sensitive computerized information at the President’s Residence was not protected in a manner suited to the risks facing a body of national importance, according to findings published on Tuesday by the State Comptroller.

item.title: State audit warns Israel’s cyber preparedness gaps threaten economy and security
item.description: Israel entered the Israel-Hamas war with major cyber-preparedness gaps, including delayed legislation, limited cabinet oversight, and weak readiness among key economic bodies. State Comptroller Matanyahu Englman warned that Israel’s cyber-preparedness gaps before and during the Israel-Hamas war amounted to a “comprehensive and significant warning,” according to a report published Tuesday on the state’s readiness for cyber incidents and its wartime performance.

item.title: Fox’s Trey Yingst Reports Shocking Details On Unprecedented Rescue of Downed Apache Helicopter Pilots
item.description: "Now, it's not clear if the gunship went down due to enemy fire or a mechanical issue."

item.title: UK pro-Palestinian protester wanted to shut factory to save lives
item.description: A pro-Palestinian protester accused of causing criminal damage to an aerospace factory has told jurors he wanted to shut the facility down for several days to try to save lives. Jurors at Birmingham Crown Court have heard four activists targeted Moog Wolverhampton Limited because they believed the business was involved in the supply chain to Israel.

item.title: Man arrested after violent stabbing in Belfast, Northern Ireland police say
item.description: Video posted to social media shows a man stabbing another man several times in the head with a knife in what appears to be an attempted beheading.

item.title: Europe Today: Zelenskyy, E3 leaders agree peace terms as pro-EU party claims victory in Armenia
item.description: The leaders of Germany, France, the UK and Ukraine agreed on concrete conditions for peace yesterday, including Europe's participation in direct talks with Vladimir Putin. In Armenia, a pro-EU party has claimed victory in the elections held yesterday.

item.title: Europe Today: Costa speaks exclusively to Euronews as EU-Western Balkans summit underway
item.description: A high-stakes EU-Western Balkans summit is underway in Montenegro, with enlargement in the spotlight and France and Germany pushing for a more gradual path to EU integration. Our EU Editor Maria Tadeo is on the ground and speaks exclusively to European Council President António Costa.

item.title: EU orders WhatsApp to restore free access for rival AI developers
item.description: The European Commission has ordered Meta's popular messaging service, WhatsApp, to grant free access to rival artificial intelligence (AI) developers until the end of an ongoing antitrust investigation. "In rapidly evolving markets, competition can be lost long before a final decision is adopted," said EU competition czar Teresa Ribera on Tuesday.

item.title: German man released in Crete following espionage allegations
item.description: A German tourist arrested in Crete on suspicion of espionage has been released. The arrest and his time in custody came as a shock to him, his wife and his daughter, the local Cretan newspaper Nea Kriti (New Crete) reported online on Tuesday, citing the man’s lawyer.

item.title: IDF kills Hezbollah terrorist who infiltrated Israeli territory, fired at soldiers
item.description: IDF sources emphasized to The Post that the terrorist did fire on IDF troops from within Israeli territory, but only having just crossed through the border fence. Despite extensive IDF forces throughout southern Lebanon, a Hezbollah terrorist on Tuesday managed to penetrate the border fence with Lebanon before IDF troops killed him.

item.title: Banger rally raises £100k for baby loss charity
item.description: Participants complete a four-day 2,500-mile journey in vehicles bought for £1,000 or less.

item.title: Get Blue aims to expand clean water access as climate change worsens global crisis
item.description: The United Nations World Water Development Report 2026 found that 2.1 billion people still lack safely managed drinking water, and the burden falls disproportionately on women and girls.

item.title: France bans Israeli Finance Minister Smotrich from entering country
item.description: France has imposed an entry ban on far-right Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich over Israel’s settlement policy in the West Bank. French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot announced on Tuesday that the entry ban was issued in connection with new sanctions that France had imposed together with the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, New Zealand and Norway over the settlement policy.

item.title: 'Operation Pushkin': six Georgians on trial in France over theft of rare Russian books
item.description: The trial is the latest case aimed at delivering justice after a series of similar thefts in libraries across Europe, allegedly by an organised network.View on euronews

item.title: Israeli air strikes hit Lebanese city of Tyre despite Iranian warning to stop attacks
item.description: Iran warned Israel on Monday that it could resume hostilities if attacks on its Lebanese ally Hezbollah do not stop.

item.title: A sexual misconduct probe against ICC's chief prosecutor says he engaged in 'serious misconduct'
item.description: An investigation into sexual misconduct allegations against the embattled chief prosecutor of the International Criminal Court found he had engaged in “serious misconduct” and “serious breach of duty,” according to documents seen by The Associated Press on Tuesday. The 21-member executive committee of the court’s oversight body, the Bureau of the Assembly of States Parties, on Monday voted to suspend British barrister Karim Khan and refer him for further disciplinary proceedings. The 56-year-old faces allegations of sexual misconduct with a female aide, in a scandal that has dragged on for more than two years.

item.title: Congo reports more than 100 Ebola deaths
item.description: The number of confirmed deaths linked to the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo has risen to 102, officials said on Tuesday. The Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC) confirmed that Ebola deaths in Congo and Uganda have risen to 102.

item.title: UK announces new sanctions on illegal Israeli settlers in West Bank
item.description: Illegal settlers in the Palestinian West Bank will be hit with new sanctions by Britain and its allies, UK Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper has told the Commons. Cooper said the “most notorious individuals” would be targeted by the measures, including “extremist figures in the Israeli Cabinet.

item.title: Russian ministry requests lifting of Roblox ban
item.description: Russia's digital ministry said on Tuesday it had asked ‌law enforcement to support lifting a ‌ban on the U.S. gaming platform Roblox RBLX.N, based ​on assurances that the company would provide extra protection for young users and comply with Russian law. The communications watchdog Roskomnadzor announced the ‌ban last December ⁠on grounds of child safety, annoying some Russian users and even ⁠sparking a rare protest in the Siberian city of Tomsk. "In connection with the guarantees of ​responsible conduct ​in the Russian ​market provided by ‌Roblox, the Ministry of Digital Development, together with Roskomnadzor, has appealed to the relevant law enforcement authorities with a request to support the lifting of restrictions," the ministry said ‌in a statement.

item.title: France bans Israeli Finance Minister Smotrich from entering country
item.description: France has imposed an entry ban on far-right Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich over Israel’s settlement policy in the West Bank. French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot announced on Tuesday that the entry ban was issued in connection with new sanctions that France had imposed together with the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, New Zealand and Norway over the settlement policy.

item.title: Afghan police disperse women's rights rally in Herat
item.description: Afghan men held a rare rally Tuesday in the western city of Herat over new restrictions against women, with police denying witness accounts to AFP that protesters were dispersed with live fire.A third witness told AFP they saw dozens of people protesting in support of women.

item.title: In Lebanon's Tyre, Christian quarter emptied out after Israeli warning
item.description: The Christian quarter in south Lebanon's Tyre, the last pocket of the coastal city spared from Israeli threats, was emptied out on Tuesday after an unprecedented warning for the city's residents.But Tuesday was the first time the Israeli army explicitly told residents of the Christian-majority neighbourhood to leave.

item.title: Hezbollah fighter crosses into Israel and is shot dead
item.description: Israeli sources say an armed member of the Lebanese Hezbollah militia has crossed into Israel and opened fire on Israeli soldiers. Troops in the Ramim Ridge area returned fire and "neutralized" the attacker, the Israeli military said in a statement on Tuesday, adding that none of the soldiers was injured.

item.title: Iran seizes assets belonging to critics
item.description: Iran's judiciary has seized more than 200 assets belonging to critics and opponents of the government, the country's judiciary spokesman said on Tuesday, calling those whose property was seized "traitors. " Property and assets were being systematically confiscated from "traitors to the fatherland," judiciary spokesman Asghar Jahangir Dozens of people have been executed in Iran since the start of the war.

item.title: Tourist Survives 7 Days Lost at Sea by Eating Raw Crabs While Clinging to Buoy: Reports
item.description: Qin Jianping reportedly fell into the ocean while walking along a cliff in South China on Wednesday, May 27

item.title: Deadly protests in Pakistan-administered Kashmir: What’s going on?
item.description: Experts say the current crisis is part of a deeper, long-running debate about governance in the region.

item.title: Lebanon minister hopes new Israel ceasefire is ‘more genuine’ than previous truce
item.description: Lebanon’s Minister of Culture Ghassane Salamé tells Europe Today that a US-brokered ceasefire with Israel offers hope of ending Israel-Hezbollah hostilities, despite previous truces breaches.

item.title: Apple blocks Siri AI from EU iPhones over DMA dispute
item.description: The European Commission rejected every compliance proposal Apple offered, including a request for an 18-month exemption from interoperability rules

item.title: Trump says in ‘final throes’ of peace deal but at least 8 killed in Lebanon
item.description: The US president warned Netanyahu he would be on his own if attacks continue after Israel and Iran paused fighting.

item.title: EU report finds drug cartels are increasingly recruiting young people
item.description: Europe’s drug gangs are increasingly using young people not only to traffic drugs but also to carry out acts of violence. The 2026 annual report by the European Union Drugs Agency (EUDA), which was published on Tuesday, says young people, particularly those from deprived neighbourhoods, are being recruited by the mafia to carry out intimidation, assaults and even to act as contract killers.

item.title: Ghanaian women defy odds to get Cambridge degrees
item.description: Scholarships help three women who grew up in poverty complete master's qualifications in the UK.

item.title: No boots, masks running out: why Congo’s Ebola medics are exposed
item.description: By Emma Farge, Jennifer Rigby, Olivia Le Poidevin and Aaron Ross NAIROBI/LONDON/GENEVA, June 9 (Reuters) - Nearly a month into one of the world’s largest ever Ebola outbreaks, medics in eastern

item.title: Venezuela cannot rebuild democracy without rebuilding its schools
item.description: Venezuela's future will not be rebuilt by oil alone. It will not be rebuilt through elections or diplomatic negotiations, necessary as those are.

item.title: Shackled, bleeding, raped: Palestinians describe abuse in Israel’s prisons
item.description: Former detainees tell Al Jazeera they were routinely sexually abused, starved and attacked by dogs.

item.title: Israel Tests Iran
item.description: Plus: Trump watches the Knicks, H-1B fee ruling, Mormons off the list, and more...

item.title: AH-64 Apache Crew Rescued By Drone Boat After Going Down Near Strait Of Hormuz
item.description: This is the first known use of a uncrewed surface vessel to recover downed aircrew and is major sign of what's to come.

item.title: Congo reports more than 100 Ebola deaths
item.description: The number of confirmed deaths linked to the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo has risen to 102, officials said on Tuesday. The Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC) confirmed that Ebola deaths in Congo and Uganda have risen to 102.

item.title: Dogs help uncover record haul of drugs hidden in lumber
item.description: The drugs found in the lumber were mostly cocaine and ketamine, with an estimated retail value of $8.3 billion, officials said.

item.title: Malawians repatriated from South Africa amid xenophobia concerns
item.description: Malawi is among several African nations transporting their citizens out of the country following reports of violence against migrants.

item.title: Why are Nigeria-South Africa tensions rising amid xenophobic attacks?
item.description: It’s not the first time the two African giants have gone head-to-head over attacks on their nationals.

item.title: EU report finds drug cartels are increasingly recruiting young people
item.description: Europe’s drug gangs are increasingly using young people not only to traffic drugs but also to carry out acts of violence. The 2026 annual report by the European Union Drugs Agency (EUDA), which was published on Tuesday, says young people, particularly those from deprived neighbourhoods, are being recruited by the mafia to carry out intimidation, assaults and even to act as contract killers.

item.title: Hedgehogs deserves banknote place, says charity
item.description: The Bank of England is asking the public to decide which animals should appear on future banknotes.

item.title: Europe faces growing synthetic opioids risks as drug market evolves, EU agency warns
item.description: PARIS/MADRID, June 9 () - Europe's illicit drug market is undergoing a rapid shift, with synthetic opioids emerging as a concern, although the ‌region still experiences far fewer fatal overdoses than North America, the ‌European Union Drugs Agency stated in its annual report. The Lisbon-based agency, drawing on data from the ​27 EU member states, Norway and Turkey, noted that at least 50 new psychoactive substances were identified for the first time in Europe in 2025.

item.title: German judge goes on trial for sexual harassment
item.description: A 56-year-old judge went on trial in central Germany on Tuesday on charges of sexually harassing a female colleague. Prosecutors believe the judge "physically touched a trainee judge in a sexually suggestive manner on multiple occasions and thereby harassed her" between September 2023 and February 2025.

item.title: Christian leaders in Lebanese city of Tyre call for quick international action after Israeli warning
item.description: Christian religious leaders from Lebanon’s southern port city of Tyre called on the international community and Lebanese officials on Tuesday to act quickly to prevent Israel from attacking the Christian district of the city, as airstrikes on nearby neighborhoods killed eight people and wounded dozens of others. The Israeli military has issued an evacuation warning for the port city, including the Christian quarter, which has been spared so far.

item.title: Man who grabbed woman's hair and asked for kiss sentenced in legal first
item.description: David Stroud was convicted in a first-of-its-kind sex-based harassment prosecution in May.

item.title: Man who murdered partner and blew up home jailed
item.description: Clifton George stabbed Annabel Rook 31 times before starting a fire at their house in north London.

item.title: Pope Leo wades into Spain's culture wars over soccer and the Catalan language in Barcelona
item.description: Pope Leo XIV waded into two of Spain's classic culture wars as he landed in Barcelona on Tuesday during his weeklong visit to the country, but sought to defuse one quickly by speaking first in the Catalan language instead of Spanish upon his arrival. The U.S.-born pontiff earlier rubbed soccer fans in Barcelona the wrong way by saying he actually roots for Real Madrid instead of their beloved Barça. Many Catalans wanted the pope to speak more Catalan publicly instead of Spanish, which Leo speaks fluently and is expected to use predominantly during his visit to Barcelona before heading to the Canary Islands.

item.title: World Cup jibes as Dutch king welcomes German president on state visit
item.description: German President Frank-Walter Steinmeier was received by Dutch King Willem-Alexander on Tuesday, with both sides alluding to the countries' fierce football rivalry ahead of the upcoming World Cup. Steinmeier began his three-day state visit to the Netherlands with his wife Elke Bündenbender, being welcomed with military honours and a 21-gun salute.

item.title: UN expert criticizes use of force against protestors in Afghanistan
item.description: A UN expert voiced alarm on Tuesday over what he described as the excessive use of force against “seemingly peaceful protesters” in the western Afghan city of Herat, following reports that demonstrators had gathered over the alleged detention of women. "It’s time to defuse the tension, respect citizens’ freedom of expression, especially women and girls, and avoid further harm.

item.title: EU increases support to tackle Ebola outbreak in Congo
item.description: The European Union on Tuesday pledged an additional €16. 5 million ($18 million) to help tackle the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

item.title: Acid attack on woman doctor sparks fear, protests in Pakistan
item.description: An acid attack against a woman doctor has sent shockwaves through Pakistan's medical sector, sparking strikes and a protest Tuesday demanding safety and justice for the victim."The incident took place due to insufficient security arrangements in the hospital, and it should thoroughly be investigated how a lift operator brings acid and throws it on a lady doctor in daylight," Hai Baloch, chairman of the Balochistan branch of the Young Doctors Association, told AFP. - 'State of fear' - Members of

item.title: Afghan protesters injured during demonstration against Taliban-imposed dress code for women
item.description: A crackdown by Afghan authorities on a protest against the arrest and detention of more than a dozen women over dress code violations reportedly left at least three people injured on Tuesday. Eyewitnesses said armed police opened fire during the protest by more than 100 people in the city of Herat. Protests are rare in Afghanistan, which has been run by the Taliban since 2021 in the wake of the chaotic withdrawal of U.S.-led forces.

item.title: From cartel prison to children's playground: the transformation of Mexican World Cup host Monterrey
item.description: By Laura Gottesdiener and Daniel Becerril MONTERREY, Mexico, June 9 (Reuters) - On a recent afternoon in northern Mexico, about a dozen children played soccer in an idyllic park on a site where, a

item.title: King Charles signs off World Cup bank holiday - but millions aren't happy
item.description: His Majesty King Charles III has given the go ahead for a last-minute bank holiday this Monday - but it sadly only applies to some

item.title: Chinese hackers pose biggest espionage threat to tech firms, CrowdStrike says
item.description: China-linked hackers posed the biggest espionage threat to technology companies over the past year,  CrowdStrike, a cybersecurity firm, said in a  report published on Tuesday, ‌amid surging investment in artificial intelligence. The hacking campaigns align with the Chinese government’s strategic priorities ‌and a sustained interest in technology development, intellectual property, and information with strategic and economic value, the firm said. The technology sector was ​once again the most targeted industry by both foreign governments and cybercriminals, the report found.

item.title: The Philippines protests China's floating 'structure' on the disputed South China Sea shoal
item.description: The Philippines has protested China’s deployment of what Manila describes as a floating “structure” with personnel on a disputed shoal in the South China Sea, fearing it could be a part of Beijing’s effort to turn the uninhabited atoll into an island base, Philippine officials said Tuesday. The Department of Foreign Affairs said, without elaborating, that it lodged a protest over China's latest action on the Scarborough Shoal, which was spotted by the Philippine military and coast guard.

item.title: A banned group’s call for a strike halts business and transport across Pakistan-administered Kashmir
item.description: Shops and businesses shut down, and public transport halted across Pakistan-administered Kashmir on Tuesday after a call for a strike by a recently banned group, known for violent protests. The Joint Awami Action Committee 's call follows clashes on Sunday in the city of Rawalakot between the group's supporters and security personnel that left seven dead. The violence erupted after the Supreme Court of Pakistan-administered Kashmir ruled that 12 legislative seats reserved for Kashmiri refugees living in Pakistan are constitutionally protected and cannot be abolished without a constitutional amendment.

item.title: Anime-sparked raccoon invasion is now wreaking havoc across Japan
item.description: "It's all because people saw this anime and decided they wanted one as a pet."

item.title: Bear that caused panic and school closures captured in Japan
item.description: The bear sightings had forced the closure of all 94 public primary and middle schools in a city just north of Tokyo.

item.title: Israel launches deadly strikes on Lebanon's Tyre after warning
item.description: BEIRUT, June 9 (Reuters) - Israel struck the historic port city of Tyre in southern Lebanon on Tuesday killing at least eight people, the Lebanese health ministry said, after an Israeli evacuation

item.title: German minister: Failure of FCAS fighter jet project 'hurts a lot'
item.description: The collapse of a highly anticipated Franco-German fighter jet project "hurts a lot," German Defence Minister Boris Pistorius said on Tuesday. "Every German-French project that is unsuccessful is one that I don't like, because I know how important Franco-German cooperation in Europe is," Pistorius said in Berlin.

item.title: Snapping turtle with bite 'like trapping your hand in car door' found at beauty spot
item.description: The turtle, which is native to parts of America, was found at Penllegare Valley Wood near Swansea.

item.title: How does the UK honours system work?
item.description: How the New Year, King's Birthday, and other UK honours are decided and awarded.

item.title: caba calls on ICAEW unpaid carers to use dedicated support service
item.description: Through the Carers Support Service, caba offers tailored help to ICAEW members.

item.title: Israel orders Lebanese city of Tyre to evacuate, ignores Iran warning
item.description: The IDF issued an evacuation order for the Tyre a day after Iran warned further attacks on Lebanon would result in "severe and crushing measures."

item.title: Several killed in Lebanon as Israel tells residents to evacuate Tyre
item.description: Several people were killed in an Israeli attack on the outskirts of the southern Lebanese coastal city of Tyre on Tuesday despite an ongoing ceasefire agreement. The Lebanese Health Ministry, citing preliminary figures, said eight people were killed in the strike and 32 wounded.

item.title: UK's last outstanding coal mine plan rejected
item.description: Plans to dig for 85,000 tonnes of coal at Glan Lash mine near Llandybie have been refused.

item.title: Kenyan police fire tear gas, make arrests at US Ebola centre protest
item.description: Kenyan protesters and police clashed Tuesday as officers fired tear gas at hundreds of people demonstrating against an Ebola quarantine centre being built for US citizens in a tourist town and made several arrests, AFP journalists saw.AFP journalists saw several people arrested by police, who also fired tear gas to disperse the growing crowds.

item.title: A 4-Year-Old Girl Was Abducted from Her Family’s Campsite. 5 Years Since Her Rescue, She’s a ‘Thriving’ Gymnast
item.description: Terence Darrell Kelly is serving over 13 years in prison for the abduction of Cleo Smith

item.title: Croc attack twins visit crocodile conservation project
item.description: Melissa Laurie was attacked when she was travelling in Mexico with her twin sister in June 2021.

item.title: Nine killed in Israeli attack on Lebanon's Tyre, sources say
item.description: At least nine people were killed and 27 wounded in an Israeli attack on the outskirts of the southern Lebanese coastal city of Tyre, despite an ongoing ceasefire agreement, Lebanese civil defence sources said on Tuesday. Shortly after the strike, the Israeli military issued an evacuation order covering the entire city, prompting fears of a wider offensive.

item.title: Drug suspects arrested after vehicle in high-speed chase hits pedestrians
item.description: One suspect drove a vehicle "at very high speed and recklessly, even hitting several local residents" before being stopped by authorities, officials said.

item.title: Tech firms in UK must have plan for illegal content spikes during crises like Southport, regulator says
item.description: LONDON, June 9 (Reuters) - Tech firms in Britain will need to have plans to respond to surges in illegal content during crises such as the 2024 Southport riots which threaten public safety under

item.title: Tech firms in UK must have plan for illegal content spikes during crises like Southport, regulator says
item.description: Tech firms in Britain will need to have plans to respond to surges in illegal ‌content during crises such as the 2024 Southport riots ‌which threaten public safety under strengthened rules set out by the regulator on Tuesday. Nationwide ​violence broke out two years ago following a knife attack in the northern England city of Southport, which left three young girls dead at a Taylor Swift-themed dance class. Authorities said inflammatory and ‌misleading content circulating widely ⁠online had fuelled that unrest.

item.title: Russia and Ukraine trade drone strikes as Zelenskyy seeks diplomatic path to end war
item.description: At least three people were killed overnight in Ukraine, a Kyiv official said.

item.title: Congo Ebola contact tracing is below target but has improved, WHO says
item.description: GENEVA, June 9 (Reuters) - Efforts to trace contacts in the Democratic Republic of Congo to try to ‌contain the country's Ebola outbreak have improved but are below ‌target, the World Health Organization said on Tuesday. There have been 550 confirmed cases of ​Ebola, including 101 deaths, according to the WHO's latest figures, as well as 94 suspected cases.

item.title: Teen charged over London arson attack on Jewish charity ambulances
item.description: 18-year-old Subhan Ahmed allegedly assisted with the torching of four ambulances used by a Jewish volunteer organization

item.title: Analysis: Chinese President Xi's silence on nuclear arms is a gift to North Korea's Kim Jong Un
item.description: Chinese and North Korean state-run media this week devoted thousands of words to Xi Jinping 's summit with Kim Jong Un, but made no mention of a key matter for Washington: the North's steadfast pursuit of nuclear weapons that could threaten the United States and its allies in Asia. Until disarmament talks finally fell apart in 2019, Washington and Beijing were yearslong partners in diplomacy seeking to persuade North Korea to abandon its nuclear ambitions in return for much-needed aid and political recognition. Beijing routinely called for “denuclearization” — a bureaucratic term for nuclear disarmament — and there was hope in Washington, as well as in Seoul and Tokyo, that China would use its perceived influence as Pyongyang's diplomatic and economic protector to push the North on the nuclear standoff.

item.title: Iran war sparks cancer drug shortages in India
item.description: Indian hospitals are running out of two life-saving cancer drugs as the war in Iran continues to disrupt the supply of raw materials.

item.title: Teen charged over Golders Green ambulance attack
item.description: Subhan Ahmed, 18, is charged with assisting an offender after four Jewish ambulances were burnt out.

item.title: Iranian citizens tell N12 they are 'hostages' of the regime, are against any deal
item.description: Any deal with the ayatollahs is a betrayal of freedom," said one Tehran resident. "They do not attach any value to the people, and are not bound by any value or agreement.

item.description: Protesters are concerned about cross-border infection risks and the lack of transparency from the government about the treatment centre.

item.description: Indonesia police allege the man killed the woman as he felt disrespected by her.

item.title: UK government 'concerned' by abuse claims against West Ham co-owner
item.description: Allegations billionaire West Ham co-owner David Sullivan preyed on women for sex are "deeply concerning", the British government said Tuesday.A spokesman added: "We will use our statutory powers to seek urgent information from David Sullivan relating to his suitability under our owners, directors and senior executives regime."

item.title: Philippines earthquake: Residents clear debris amid concerns over aftershocks
item.description: The Philippines is assessing the impact of a powerful earthquake that struck Mindanao on 8 June. Residents are clearing debris while hospitals continue operating outdoors amid concerns over aftershocks.

item.title: What we know about Xi's visit to North Korea
item.description: President Xi Jinping concluded a visit to North Korea on Tuesday, after meetings with Kim Jong Un that the Chinese leader said reached an "important consensus" on building ties.- Xi said he had reached "an important consensus with Kim on developing China-DPRK relations in the new era", China's Xinhua news agency reported, using North Korea's official acronym.

item.title: Kenyan police fire tear gas, make arrests at US Ebola centre protest
item.description: Kenyan protesters and police clashed Tuesday as officers fired tear gas at hundreds of people demonstrating against an Ebola quarantine centre being built for US citizens in a tourist town and made several arrests, AFP journalists saw.AFP journalists saw several people arrested by police, who also fired tear gas to disperse the growing crowds.

item.title: Germany turns net electricity exporter as wind power boosts production
item.description: Germany was a net exporter of electricity in the first quarter of the year for the first time since late 2023, official data showed on Tuesday, with wind proving the single biggest source of generation. Electricity imports fell by 15.

item.title: Why a Ukrainian mobile air defense unit isn't ready to give up its nearly century-old machine gun
item.description: The unit is training to operate high-tech interceptor drones that have become popular in Ukraine; however, their old weapons still do the trick.

item.description: By Edwin Okoth NANYUKI, Kenya, June 9 (Reuters) - Kenyan police fired tear gas on Tuesday and detained protesters demonstrating against a quarantine centre for Americans exposed to Ebola that the U.S.

item.title: UN: Israeli settler violence against Palestinians in West Bank rising
item.description: Violence by Israeli settlers against Palestinian civilians in the West Bank continues to escalate, a report released on Tuesday by the UN Independent International Commission of Inquiry shows. Last year, settlers killed at least seven Palestinians and injured 832 others, meaning that the number of victims has more than doubled compared to 2024, according to the report, which was presented to the UN Human Rights Council.

item.title: Black bear caught in Japan after days of sightings
item.description: The capture in Utsunomiya comes as bear attacks have reached record levels across Japan.

item.title: Zelenskyy arrives in Estonia to attend Nordic-Baltic summit
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy arrived in Estonia on Tuesday to attend a summit of Nordic and Baltic leaders and to meet with Estonian President Alar Karis, Estonian public broadcaster ERR reported. Estonia is hosting the summit in its capital, Tallinn. The country holds the rotating presidency of the NB8, a regional grouping of the five Nordic countries and the three Baltic states.

item.title: Germany records uptick in violent crimes with political motive
item.description: Germany recorded a rise in politically motivated crimes in 2025, with far-right circles responsible for the most violent offences. Police registered a total of 85,837 offences with a political motivation last year, almost 2% more than in 2024.

item.title: Congo reports more than 100 Ebola deaths
item.description: The number of confirmed deaths linked to the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo has risen to 101, officials said on Tuesday. According to a statement by the Congolese Ministry of Information on X, a total of 550 cases had been confirmed as of Sunday.

item.title: Residents flee Lebanese city after Israeli warning as strikes reportedly kill three
item.description: The Israeli military issues evacuation order for the southern city of Tyre - including the Christian quarter - saying it will act "forcefully".

item.description: Violence by Israeli settlers against Palestinian civilians in the West Bank continues to escalate, a report released on Tuesday by the UN Independent International Commission of Inquiry shows. Last year, settlers killed at least seven Palestinians and injured 832 others, meaning that the number of victims has more than doubled compared to 2024, according to the report, which was presented to the UN Human Rights Council.

item.title: Fireworks factory blast in Malta misrepresented as US, Iran strikes
item.description: Missile and drone strikes in early June 2026 strained the United States and Iran's fragile ceasefire, but footage of a massive explosion circulating online is unrelated to the Middle East war. Social media posts are claiming the blast was either at an Iranian air force base hit by US strikes or in Kuwait after an Iranian attack, but it in fact occurred at a fireworks factory in Malta on June 1.

item.title: UK retail warns over flexible jobs reforms
item.description: The British Retail Consortium says proposed guaranteed hours rules could unintentionally limit flexible work in UK retail.

item.title: U.N. court chief prosecutor suspended over sexual misconduct allegations
item.description: Embattled International Criminal Court chief prosecutor Karim Khan was suspended after the court's oversight body referred him for disciplinary proceedings.

item.description: China's President travelled to North Korea for the first time in seven years, underscoring the strategic importance of ties between the two neighbours.

item.title: International alliance unveils reusable packaging logo
item.description: An international alliance has launched a global symbol to identify verified reusable packaging systems.

item.title: Moscow car bomb kills 1 person, investigators say, with victim yet to be identified
item.description: The bomb exploded early on Tuesday in the Moscow suburb of Balashikha.

item.title: Protests erupt in Kenya over US Ebola quarantine centre in Nanyuki
item.description: Anger grows in Kenya as residents accuse the US of offloading Ebola risks onto Kenyans.

item.title: ‘They were laughing’: Israel’s use of rape and sexual abuse in prisons
item.description: Former detainees detail systematic torture and sexual violence, including rape, while in Israeli custody.

item.title: Belgium: Thousands protest alleged police violence in Brussels
item.description: Large crowds gathered in Brussels on 8 June to condemn what protesters described as excessive police force during recent demonstrations. The dispute is rooted in planned spending cuts affecting French-speaking education in Belgium

item.title: New health risks emerge as Europe’s drug market grows more complex, EU agency warns
item.description: Europe’s drug market is growing more complex, with potent opioids, stronger cannabis and new substances raising health risks, the EU agency warns.

item.title: Why China’s Xi wants a ‘brighter’ future with North Korea
item.description: Chinese leader Xi Jinping’s first visit to North Korea in seven years wasn’t just loaded with rhetoric hailing historic ties between China and its longstanding – and only – treaty ally.

item.title: Cuba: Powerful offshore earthquake shakes Havana, prompting office evacuation
item.description: A powerful earthquake struck western Cuba on 8 June, sending people rushing out of buildings in Havana. Witnesses described scenes of confusion as offices were evacuated and workers waited outside for safety checks.

item.title: Crimea bridge damaged by Ukrainian attacks for second night in a row
item.description: Ukrainian drones have damaged a key bridge connecting Russian-controlled Crimea with the mainland for the second consecutive night, Russian officials said on Tuesday. The Chonhar bridge, which links the Black Sea peninsula with the Russian-occupied Ukrainian region of Kherson, the Russian-installed administrative chief of Kherson Vladimir Saldo said on Telegram.

item.title: Eviction drive footage in Bangladesh misrepresented as migrants ejected from home in India
item.description: India's Hindu-nationalist Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) ordered authorities to step up enforcement on illegal migration after winning power in West Bengal in May 2026, but a clip circulating online does not show migrants forced from their homes in the state. It was actually filmed in neighbouring Bangladesh during an operation to evict locals who had allegedly built houses on government land in the capital Dhaka without approval.

item.title: Japanese city closes nearly 100 schools after bear sightings as post-hibernation attacks rise

item.title: Italian commuters find a moment of peace on a cable-guided ferry sketched by Leonardo da Vinci
item.description: The ferry glides from one bank of northern Italy's Adda River to the other, guided by a cable and pulled by currents, offering harried commuters five minutes of serenity and an alternate route now that a bridge closure has backed up traffic. It is the last remaining of its kind along the Adda River, which extends from the Alps to the Po River in the Lombardy region. “This is a mean of transport that has been here for 500 years and has always connected the two banks of the Adda,” said Massimo Zoia, one of the volunteer ferrymen who operates the vessel.

item.title: Survivors share experiences and lessons from Congo’s 2018 Ebola outbreak
item.description: The memories come flooding back whenever Vianney Kambale Kombi hears the word Ebola. Kombi also remembers the broad skepticism over the disease, attacks on health workers and inaction from patients that he blames for the speed in which the disease spread. “We thought it was witchcraft,” said Kombi.

item.title: Trump says pilots are fine after US helicopter crashes near Strait of Hormuz
item.description: A U.S. Army Apache attack helicopter crashed near the Strait of Hormuz, with President Donald Trump saying the two crew members on board were “fine” after the incident involving the strategic waterway, which remains under a chokehold by Iran. What caused the crash remained unclear Tuesday morning in the Middle East, which was still reeling after Iran and Israel exchanged fire the previous day in the biggest blow yet to the straining ceasefire in the Iran war. Since the U.S. and Israel began striking Iran on Feb. 28, the war has shaken the global economy, driven up energy prices around the world and made many basics, including food, more expensive.

item.title: Israel kills 14 in southern Lebanon after trading fire with Iran

item.title: 20,000 people displaced by the Philippine earthquake that killed at least 37
item.description: Rescuers searched ruined buildings in the southern Philippines on Tuesday to ensure no one was still trapped a day after one of the strongest earthquakes to hit the country in a half-century killed at least 37 people and displaced more than 20,000. Only four people were considered missing on official records in the southern provinces near where the 7.8 magnitude quake struck Monday morning, but the Office of Civil Defense acknowledged several collapsed and heavily damaged buildings must be thoroughly inspected for possible survivors or casualties. The earthquake centered off Mindanao, the second most populous Philippine island, injured nearly 500 people and displaced more than 20,000, most of whom fled to emergency shelters.

item.title: Sweden set to ban mobile phones in schools, joining trend of shelving screens for students
item.description: MALMÖ, Sweden (AP) — Long championed as a leader in adopting digital technology, Sweden is set to ban mobile phones in schools beginning in the fall for the next academic year as part of a broad, international reversal on the use of screens in classrooms. Since 2023, the Scandinavian country’s center-right coalition government has pursued a policy prioritizing more reading time and less screen time, particularly among preschool students, by favoring books and other traditional learning tools. Lawmaker Joar Forsell, chairperson of the Swedish parliament's education committee, said officials have seen a decline in the general ability to read and write in Sweden, especially among younger students.

Last week, Sen. Dan Sullivan accused the challenger Sullivan of “trying to trick” voters to help his main rival in the race, Democratic former U.S. Rep. Mary Peltola.

item.title: Protesters, police clash in Bolivia after president signs law enabling a harsher crackdown
item.description: Protesters in Bolivia demanding the resignation of conservative President Rodrigo Paz hurled firecrackers, stones and sticks at police who responded with tear gas on Monday, leading to dozens of arrests as road blockades continue to paralyze the Andean nation. The renewed clashes in the central city of Cochabamba erupted after President Paz signed a measure that could pave the way for a hard-line government crackdown on the demonstrations that have roiled Bolivia over the past five weeks, disrupting transportation and causing shortages of food, fuel and medical supplies. Bolivia's national workers' union, peasant farmers and Indigenous groups from the highlands — outraged over Paz's scrapping of fuel subsidies and frustrated with his failure in the last seven months to resolve Bolivia's persistent economic problems — have set up 90 blockades on key routes nationwide, effectively isolating major cities, especially La Paz, the seat of government and neighboring El Alto.

item.title: Trump administration will offer expedited visa interviews at select embassies for $750
item.description: The State Department will offer a “premium” expedited service for foreigners seeking business or tourist visas to come to the United States that will set applicants back $750 — on top of the initial fee of $185. In a notice to be published in the Federal Register this week, the department will unveil a pilot program that will allow visa applicants to pay the $750 to schedule an appointment for an interview within 10 days of the payment at select U.S. embassies and consulates. The pilot program will run from July 1 to Dec. 31, according to internal documents obtained by The Associated Press and a State Department official, who spoke on condition of anonymity because the program has not yet been announced.

item.title: Man who killed O’Shae Sibley after he danced at a Brooklyn gas station is convicted of manslaughter
item.description: A jury convicted a man of manslaughter as a hate crime on Monday in the death of O’Shae Sibley, who was killed at a Brooklyn gas station during a confrontation that began with a group of young people shouting racist and anti-gay slurs at the professional dancer and his friends as they vogued to a Beyoncé song. Dmitriy Popov, 20, who was 17 at the time of the killing, testified at trial that he was just defending himself when he stabbed Sibley, 28, in 2023. Prosecutors said Popov acted out of hate, taunting and jeering at Sibley, then killing him when he reacted to the provocations.

In Tel Aviv, Israel’s commercial and cultural hub, the usually bustling streets were more subdued than normal, with fewer customers at shops and other businesses than regular weekdays. Hours earlier, missiles were launched from both Yemen and Iran toward Israel.

item.title: Gordon S. Wood, eminent scholar of the American Revolution, dead at 92
item.description: Gordon S. Wood, the eminent and prolific scholar who forged a highly influential and sharply debated narrative of the country’s early years of independence through such prize-winning works as “The Creation of the American Republic” and “The Radicalism of the American Revolution,” has died. Wood, a professor emeritus at Brown University, died Sunday after being struck by a car in a supermarket parking lot in East Providence, Rhode Island, according to police. Author of dozens of books and essays, Wood never gained the mass audience of historians like David McCullough and Doris Kearns Goodwin, but his findings became standard references for discussions about the formation of the U.S. and the legacy of the revolution.

item.title: Antibiotic resistance genes found across world oceans, Italian study shows
item.description: ROME, June 8 () - Genes linked to antibiotic resistance are present across multiple ocean basins, including remote waters, according to findings ‌released on Monday from an Italian-led research project that analysed seawater ‌samples worldwide. The SeA Care project found antibiotic-resistance genes in the Mediterranean, Atlantic, Arctic and other ​regions, with higher concentrations detected near busy shipping routes and densely populated coastal areas.

item.title: Ukrainian mothers see a rising number of complications with their pregnancies, in photos

Population Fund.

item.title: Celebrating a wedding amid the Ebola outbreak: No kisses or close contact, but love lives here
item.description: There were no kisses, long embraces or a crowded dance floor packed with well-wishers. As Congo is battling an Ebola disease outbreak that has killed nearly 100 people out of the more than 500 confirmed cases, local authorities have raced to slow the disease with strict measures, including by limiting public gatherings and enforcing social distancing. For Jean Claude Érable and his bride Solange Hahati, celebrating their wedding on Saturday in such conditions meant having some family members and friends absent on one of their happiest days.

item.title: In first papal speech to Spanish parliament, pope demands respect for migrants and international law
item.description: Pope Leo XIV called Monday for newfound respect for the rights of migrants and international law during an address to the Spanish parliament that signaled a new level of acceptance of the Catholic Church in the public sphere in the overwhelmingly secular country. In the first papal address to the Spanish legislature, the American pope said a “moral renewal” was necessary in legislatures and public life to ensure respect for the inherent dignity of all people, including migrants, the unborn and the most vulnerable. Pope Francis addressed a joint session of the U.S. Congress in 2015, and Pope Benedict XVI addressed his native German Bundestag in 2011.
